text: The surgeon inspects the pelvis for endometriosis lesions on the bowel, punch of the glass, and uterosacral ligament. Cold scissors are used to open the ovarian cyst at the anti-mesial point, creating a three-centimeter incision. Menstrual blood is removed from the cyst, followed by a cystoscopy to check for vegetation inside the washed cyst. No vegetation is observed, allowing the cystectomy to proceed.

The surgeon performs traction and counter-traction on the ovarian cyst, carefully manipulating the tissue centimeter by centimeter to minimize damage to the ovary. The procedure avoids using energy to reduce the risk of ovarian failure.

The surgeon uses two devices to gently traction and counter-traction each part of the cyst and ovary, progressing centimeter by centimeter. The dissection is bloodless as they remain in level two of the cyst, approaching the hilum. The surgeon carefully tracks the plane between the cyst and ovary, aiming to remove the cyst from the ovarian cortex smoothly, controlling any bleeding by compression.

The surgeon uses two closely positioned devices to perform traction and counter-traction on the cyst, carefully adjusting the axis of traction to avoid damaging the ovarian cortex. The technique aims for a precise, bloodless dissection to preserve fertility.

The surgeon and assistant perform controlled traction and counter-traction near the hilum, revealing capillary bleeding. The surgeon tracks the cyst while the assistant tracks the ovarian cortex, clearly exposing the plane between the cyst and the ovarian cortex. A follicle is visible, indicating precise dissection in the final stage of the cystectomy.

The surgeon uses two devices to apply very close traction between the ovary and the cyst, adjusting the axis of traction to improve visibility of the working plane. Gentle traction is applied to navigate strong adhesions between the cyst and ovary, aiming to preserve ovarian function.

The surgeon extracts a large nine-centimeter cyst through a twelve-millimeter trocar. Hemostasis is achieved using a monofilament stitch, specifically a monocryl stitch, to control bleeding from the cystectomy site.

The surgeon performs a suture on the edge of the ovary, ensuring the knot is tied gently to prevent ischemia. The stitches are placed to maintain contact between the two faces of the ovary, reducing the risk of intra-ovarian hematoma. The intracorporeal knotting technique is used, followed by cutting the stitch and performing peritoneal washing.
